document.name: PCI DSS v4.0.1 SAQ D for Merchants
notes: This is a merchant-friendly PRD JSON derived from SAQ D for Merchants.
It is not a verbatim replacement for PCI DSS text.
Use this for product UX, checklist generation, scoring, and remediation flows.
Use the original PCI document for legal/compliance validation.

sections.title: Firewalls & Network Security Controls
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Protect the cardholder data environment with firewalls, approved connections, restricted traffic, and reviewed network rules.
questions: Do you have network security controls such as firewalls protecting your cardholder data environment?
Are firewall and network security rules documented, approved, and kept up to date?
Do you maintain an accurate network diagram showing cardholder-data systems and connections?
Do you maintain a data-flow diagram showing where account data travels across systems and networks?
Are only necessary services, ports, and protocols allowed?
Are insecure protocols protected with additional security measures or removed?
Are network security control rules reviewed at least every six months?
Are wireless networks separated from the cardholder data environment unless explicitly authorized?
Is inbound and outbound traffic restricted to only what is required for business?
Are anti-spoofing controls in place to block forged source IP addresses?
Are systems that store card data blocked from direct access from untrusted networks?
Are laptops or other devices that connect to both the internet and the cardholder environment secured with enforced controls?

sections.title: Secure System Configurations
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Use hardened configurations, change default settings, disable unnecessary functions, and protect admin access.
questions: Do you have secure configuration standards for all systems in scope?
Are vendor default passwords and accounts changed, disabled, or removed before production use?
Are systems hardened using industry or vendor recommendations?
Do systems run only the services, protocols, and functions that are actually needed?
Are systems with different security levels separated or secured to the highest required level?
Are insecure services or protocols documented with business justification and compensating protections?
Are system security settings configured to prevent misuse?
Is all non-console administrative access encrypted using strong cryptography?
If wireless is used, are wireless defaults changed and encryption keys rotated when needed?

sections.title: Stored Card Data Protection
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Store as little account data as possible, never retain sensitive authentication data after authorization, and protect any stored PAN with strong controls and key management.
questions: Do you keep stored account data to the absolute minimum needed for legal, regulatory, or business reasons?
Do you have a documented retention and deletion policy for account data across all locations?
Do you verify at least every three months that expired stored account data is securely deleted or made unrecoverable?
Do you avoid storing sensitive authentication data after authorization, even if encrypted?
Do you prevent storage of full track data after authorization?
Do you prevent storage of card verification codes after authorization?
Do you prevent storage of PINs or PIN blocks after authorization?
If sensitive authentication data is stored before authorization, is it encrypted with strong cryptography?
Is the full PAN unreadable wherever it is stored, such as via truncation, strong encryption, tokens, or approved hashing?
If hashes are used for PAN, are they keyed cryptographic hashes with proper key management?
If disk or partition encryption is used, is PAN also protected appropriately where required?
Is full PAN masked when displayed so only authorized users can see more than the allowed digits?
Do remote access tools prevent unauthorized copying or relocation of PAN?
Are encryption keys for stored account data protected against disclosure and misuse?
Are secret and private keys stored securely in approved forms?
Is cleartext access to key components limited to the fewest custodians necessary?
Do you manage cryptographic keys securely throughout their lifecycle, including generation, distribution, storage, rotation, retirement, and protection against unauthorized substitution?

sections.title: Encryption During Transmission
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Encrypt cardholder data whenever it travels across open or public networks, and avoid insecure transmission methods.
questions: Is cardholder data encrypted with strong cryptography whenever it is transmitted over open or public networks?
Do you use only trusted, secure protocols and configurations for transmitting account data?
Are insecure protocols removed or protected with strong compensating controls?
Do you prevent PAN from being sent through end-user messaging technologies such as email, chat, or SMS unless explicitly protected and allowed?

sections.title: Malware Protection
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Protect systems from malware using anti-malware tools or equivalent controls, and keep protections active and current.
questions: Are systems commonly affected by malware protected with anti-malware tools or equivalent controls?
Are malware protections kept current, actively running, and generating audit logs?
Are periodic malware scans performed when continuous protection is not used?
Can users avoid or disable anti-malware protections only with documented management approval?

sections.title: Secure Software Development & Patching
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Identify vulnerabilities, apply patches, and build or change software using secure development practices.
questions: Do you identify and rank security vulnerabilities based on risk?
Are critical and high-risk security patches installed within required timeframes?
Are custom and bespoke applications developed using secure coding practices?
Are code changes reviewed, tested, and approved before production release?
Are public-facing applications protected against common attacks such as injection and script-based attacks?
Do change-control processes cover all production system changes?

sections.title: Access Control by Business Need
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Grant access only to the people and systems that need it, and keep privileges limited and reviewed.
questions: Is access to systems and cardholder data limited to what each user needs for their role?
Are roles and access responsibilities documented and understood?
Are privileged accounts tightly controlled and justified?
Are access rights reviewed regularly and removed when no longer needed?

sections.title: User Identification & Authentication
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Give each user a unique identity, require strong authentication, and protect login credentials.
questions: Does every user have a unique ID for access to systems in scope?
Are strong passwords or passphrases required and managed securely?
Is multi-factor authentication used where required, especially for administrative and remote access?
Are authentication factors protected from disclosure and misuse?
Are dormant, shared, generic, and service accounts controlled appropriately?

sections.title: Physical Security
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Restrict physical access to cardholder data, media, systems, and sensitive areas.
questions: Is physical access to cardholder-data areas restricted to authorized personnel?
Are visitors identified, authorized, logged, and escorted where required?
Are paper records and media containing cardholder data physically secured?
Are devices inspected and protected against tampering or substitution where applicable?
Is media securely destroyed when no longer needed?

sections.title: Logging & Monitoring
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Log important events, protect logs from tampering, and review them so suspicious activity is detected quickly.
questions: Are audit logs enabled for access to systems and cardholder data?
Do logs capture user activity, admin actions, authentication events, and security-relevant changes?
Are logs protected from unauthorized modification or deletion?
Are logs reviewed daily or according to risk for suspicious activity?
Are logs retained for the required retention period, including at least one year where required?

sections.title: Security Testing
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Regularly test systems and networks using scans, penetration tests, and change-detection methods.
questions: Do you run internal and external vulnerability scans as required?
Are ASV scans performed where applicable?
Do you perform penetration testing at required intervals and after significant changes?
Are wireless environments tested for unauthorized devices if wireless is present?
Do you use file-integrity or change-detection mechanisms where required?
Are security issues found during testing tracked and remediated?

sections.title: Security Policies, Risk, Vendors & Incident Response
sections.merchant_friendly_summary: Maintain governance, training, risk management, vendor oversight, and a workable incident response process.
questions: Do you maintain and review an information security policy at least annually?
Are security responsibilities assigned and understood across the organization?
Are employees trained on security awareness when hired and regularly after that?
Do you perform risk assessments and use them to guide your security program?
Do you maintain an inventory and oversight process for third-party service providers?
Do you have an incident response plan that is tested and kept current?
Are PCI responsibilities for service providers clearly documented and monitored?
